name: Hwasong-17 (Paektusan-2)
detail: DPRK's largest intercontinental ballistic missile, first successfully tested November 18, 2022. Lofted trajectory reached ~6,000 km altitude; estimated maximum range 15,000+ km, sufficient to strike any US city. Liquid-fueled, transported on 11-axle TEL (transporter-erector-launcher). Yield of nuclear warhead estimated 100–300 kt.

name: Hwasong-15
detail: First tested November 28, 2017. Lofted trajectory of ~4,500 km altitude; estimated max range ~13,000 km. Kim Jong-un declared it capable of striking the entire US mainland. DPRK paraded multiple Hwasong-15s in 2018 and 2020. Liquid-fueled with separating warhead.

name: Hwasong-14
detail: DPRK's first ICBM, tested twice on July 4 and July 28, 2017. Second test reached ~3,700 km altitude and demonstrated range of approximately 10,000 km. Liquid-fueled; US analysts estimated it could reach Alaska and the Pacific Northwest of the continental US.

name: Hwasong-12
detail: Intermediate-range ballistic missile, tested successfully May and August 2017. The August 29, 2017 test flew over Hokkaido, Japan, triggering J-Alert emergency warnings. Range approximately 4,500–5,000 km, sufficient to reach Guam. Liquid-fueled single-stage missile.

name: KN-23 (Hwasong-11)
detail: Short-range ballistic missile first tested in 2019. Similar in profile to Russia's Iskander-M. Solid-fueled, road-mobile, low-altitude depressed trajectory designed to evade missile defense. Range ~450–690 km. South Korea confirmed DPRK transferred KN-23 missiles to Russia for use in Ukraine, with examples recovered on Ukrainian battlefields in 2024.

name: Pukguksong-3
detail: Submarine-launched ballistic missile, tested from underwater platform October 2019. Solid-fueled, cold-launch ejection system. Range estimated 1,900+ km. Advanced version of the Pukguksong-1 (first tested 2016). DPRK unveiled the Pukguksong-5 at October 2021 parade. Second-strike capability is the stated purpose but operational submarine carrier is not yet confirmed as reliable.

name: Yongbyon 5MWe Graphite Reactor & Uranium Enrichment Facility
detail: DPRK's primary plutonium production reactor, operational since 1986. Produces approximately 6 kg of weapons-grade plutonium per year when running. IAEA satellite analysis confirmed the reactor resumed operations in 2021 after a hiatus. The adjacent centrifuge enrichment facility (revealed to US scientist Sig Hecker in 2010) is capable of producing highly-enriched uranium. IAEA inspectors expelled in 2009.

name: Long-Range Artillery (170mm Koksan & 240mm MLRS)
detail: DPRK deploys approximately 8,000–10,000 artillery pieces along the DMZ, including 170mm Koksan self-propelled guns (range ~60 km) and 240mm multiple launch rocket systems capable of reaching Seoul and US military bases at Camp Humphreys. These constitute the primary conventional threat to South Korea and a key instrument of deterrence against US strikes.

name: Lazarus Group / Bureau 121 (RGB Cyber Unit)
detail: North Korea's state-sponsored cyber warfare division, operating under the Reconnaissance General Bureau (RGB). Responsible for the 2014 Sony Pictures hack, 2016 Bangladesh Bank heist ($81M stolen), 2017 WannaCry ransomware (150 countries affected), and over $3 billion in cryptocurrency theft from 2022–2024. The most prolific state-sponsored cybercrime operation in the world.

name: DPRK Submarine Fleet (~70 vessels)
detail: North Korea operates the world's largest submarine fleet by numbers, including approximately 20+ Romeo-class submarines, 40+ Sang-O/Yugo-class coastal submarines, and at least one new large submarine unveiled in 2023. The Romeo-class carries conventional torpedoes; the new submarine is claimed to be capable of carrying SLBMs. Much of the fleet is obsolete; primary threat is mini-submarine infiltration and torpedo attacks on surface vessels, as demonstrated in the 2010 Cheonan sinking.

name: Chemical Weapons Stockpile (estimated 2,500–5,000 tonnes)
detail: DPRK is assessed by US intelligence to possess one of the world's largest chemical weapons stockpiles, including mustard gas, sarin, VX, tabun, and hydrogen cyanide. DPRK never joined the Chemical Weapons Convention. VX nerve agent was used in the 2017 assassination of Kim Jong-nam at Kuala Lumpur airport, demonstrating operational capability. DPRK also assessed to maintain biological weapons research programs.

name: KPA Special Operations Forces (~200,000)
detail: North Korea's special operations command is among the largest in the world, comprising light infantry, reconnaissance, and infiltration units. Designed for rapid penetration of South Korean defenses, sabotage of US military installations, and destabilization operations. Deployed to Russia in 2024 as the first documented overseas combat deployment. Includes parachute and amphibious assault brigades.
